title: AML / ComplyAdvantage screening not triggered
description: ComplyAdvantage screening has not been run on any party connected to this application. Under §31.5, sanctions screening is mandatory for all applicants, directors, guarantors, and beneficial owners before the case can progress past FMA stage. A sanctions match is a mandatory decline (Policy Limit *(L)*). PEP status triggers Enhanced Due Diligence and Senior Management sign-off — neither can be obtained if screening has not been run. Separately, §28.9 requires the source of the purchase deposit to be fully evidenced and AML-reviewed before Offer; this cannot be completed without the screening framework being in place. Until ComplyAdvantage returns clear results for Hartwell Properties Ltd, Rachel Bennett (Director/Guarantor), and all beneficial owners, this case is blocked from progression.

rows.label: Parties requiring screening
rows.value: Hartwell Properties Ltd; Rachel Bennett (Director / Guarantor)

rows.label: Policy requirement — sanctions
rows.value: §31.5 — mandatory before progression past FMA; sanctions match = decline (L)

rows.label: Policy requirement — PEP
rows.value: §31.5 — PEP requires Enhanced Due Diligence + Senior Management sign-off

rows.label: Policy requirement — source of deposit
rows.value: §28.9 — AML review of deposit source required before Offer

title: Director DOB mismatch — Director's search cannot be relied upon
description: Rachel Bennett's date of birth as stated on the Full Mortgage Application (FMA) is 14 March 1982. The Companies House filing for Hartwell Properties Ltd records her DOB as 14 March 1983 — a difference of exactly one year. This is not a formatting discrepancy; it is a data conflict between two primary sources. Under §30.3a, a Director's search must be conducted on each Guarantor before Offer, and the results must positively confirm: (a) no directorship of a company liquidated in the last 3 years, and (b) no current or historical director disqualification. Both conditions are Policy Limits *(L)*. A Director's search result is only reliable if it has been run against the correct identity. While the DOB conflict remains unresolved, it is not possible to confirm the search has matched to the correct individual — meaning the *(L)* gate at §30.3a cannot be cleared. Rachel Bennett must provide documentary evidence of her true date of birth (passport or birth certificate) and the discrepancy must be resolved before any Director's search result can be accepted as satisfying the §30.3a requirement.

rows.label: Policy gate
rows.value: §30.3a — Director's search mandatory per guarantor; no liquidation (last 3 yrs), no disqualification (L)

rows.label: Implication
rows.value: DOB conflict means search results cannot be relied upon to clear the §30.3a (L) gate

actions: Request passport / birth certificate from Rachel Bennett
Resolve DOB before running Director's search

title: ICR FAILS at correct stressed rate — Policy Limit breach
description: The case has been presented with a stressed ICR of 142% against a threshold of 140%, both calculated using a 7.5% stress rate. This is incorrect. Under §24.2, the minimum stressed interest rate for a fixed-rate product with a term of less than 5 years (which the 2-year Fixed product is) is 5.50% for all borrowers. The exception that permits a 5.00% rate applies only to individual borrowers remortgaging a property they owned personally before 6 April 2017 with no capital raising — this is a purchase application by an SPV, so the exception does not apply. At the correct 5.50% rate, the annual stressed interest cost is £214,200 × 5.50% = £11,781. Against stated monthly rent of £1,200 (£14,400/year), the ICR is £14,400 / £11,781 = 122.2%. The minimum ICR for a Limited Company borrower under §24.1 is 125%. The case fails the ICR test by approximately 2.8 percentage points on the most favourable rental assumption. This is a Policy Limit *(L)* breach — it cannot be approved by a mandate holder alone and requires Credit Committee approval plus relevant Stakeholder sign-off under §14.2.

rows.label: Stress rate used in DIP
rows.value: 7.5% (incorrect — not the policy rate for this product/borrower)

rows.label: Approval route for Policy Limit breach

title: LTV 75.2% exceeds 75% product maximum — Policy Limit
description: The loan of £214,200 against an estimated property value of £285,000 gives an LTV of 75.2%, which exceeds the 75% maximum LTV for the 2-year Fixed BTL product. Under §21, LTV on a purchase is calculated against the lower of purchase price or valuation — the formal valuation has not yet been received. If the valuer assesses the property at less than £285,000, the LTV breach will widen further. Even at the current estimated value, the LTV is in breach. A reduction of the loan to £213,750 or below (£285,000 × 75%) would bring LTV within the product limit — but given the ICR is already failing (see sug_003), a loan reduction alone does not resolve the case. This is a Policy Limit *(L)* under §22 and §23. Per §14.2, Credit Committee approval and Stakeholder sign-off are required to proceed with a loan above the product's maximum LTV. The case should not proceed to formal valuation instruction until the LTV and ICR positions are addressed.

rows.label: Valuation basis (§21)
rows.value: Lower of purchase price or valuation — formal valuation not yet received

rows.label: Risk if valuation below estimate
rows.value: LTV breach will widen if value < £285,000

rows.label: Policy reference
rows.value: §22 loan parameters; §23 obligor exposure and LTV; §14.2 Credit Committee for (L) breaches

actions: Review loan amount vs 75% LTV cap

title: Guarantor shareholding verification required — §30.3a
description: Under §30.3a, for a Limited Company borrower: (a) all Shareholders holding ≥25% controlling interest must provide personal guarantees; (b) guarantors must collectively hold an aggregate controlling interest of ≥60%. To date, only Rachel Bennett has been identified as Director and Guarantor. The Companies House confirmation statement for Hartwell Properties Ltd has not yet been reviewed. Until it is reviewed, it is not possible to confirm whether any other shareholders hold ≥25% — if they do, they must also provide personal guarantees before Offer. It is also not possible to confirm the aggregate guarantor controlling interest meets the ≥60% threshold. Both conditions are Policy Limits *(L)* — the case cannot proceed to Offer without them being positively confirmed. Retrieving and reviewing the confirmation statement is an immediate required action.

rows.label: Policy requirement (§30.3a)
rows.value: All ≥25% shareholders must guarantee; aggregate ≥60% controlling interest required (L)

actions: Obtain and review Companies House confirmation statement
Request guarantees from any additional qualifying shareholders

title: Tenancy documentation must comply with RRA 2025 APT framework
description: Under §43 (updated in policy v13, April 2026), from 1 May 2026 Assured Shorthold Tenancies (ASTs) have been replaced by Assured Periodic Tenancies (APT) under the Renters Rights Act 2025 (RRA 2025). This change is already in force — today's date is 19 May 2026. The tenancy documentation for 27 Belmont Road, Worcester has not yet been received. When it is received, it must be reviewed to ensure compliance: if the document is dated on or after 1 May 2026, it must be in APT form under RRA 2025 — a legacy AST template will not be acceptable. If the document is dated before 1 May 2026 (i.e., an existing AST), the underwriter must confirm the tenancy has transitioned to APT status as required by the Act and that no legacy AST-only provisions are being relied upon. Standard AST wording used by many managing agents has not automatically been updated — this is a practical risk. The tenancy document must be specifically checked for RRA 2025 compliance before the case proceeds to Offer.

rows.label: RRA 2025 effective date
rows.value: 1 May 2026 — ASTs replaced by APTs from this date

rows.label: Tenancy documentation status
rows.value: Not yet received — outstanding from agent

rows.label: Risk if pre-1 May 2026 document
rows.value: Must confirm transition to APT — legacy fixed-term AST clauses no longer enforceable

rows.label: Risk if post-1 May 2026 document
rows.value: Must be drafted as APT under RRA 2025 — standard legacy AST templates not acceptable

actions: Review document for RRA 2025 APT compliance
Confirm transition from AST if pre-1 May 2026

title: EPC certificate not on file — hard gate before Offer
description: Under §46, all security properties must have a minimum Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) rating of E before MT Finance can issue an Offer. This is a Policy Limit *(L)*. No EPC certificate has been sighted or uploaded for 27 Belmont Road, Worcester. The property cannot proceed to Offer without evidence of at least an E rating. If the EPC is rated F or G, the case must be declined unless the applicant provides a credible, evidenced improvement plan demonstrating the property will be brought to at least E before it is let — and this improvement must be completed and re-certified before drawdown. EPC certificates are valid for 10 years from the date of issue; if an existing certificate is located, its expiry date must be confirmed. Given that the property is a purchase, an EPC should be available from the estate agent particulars or the Energy Performance of Buildings Register. Obtaining and reviewing the EPC is an immediate required action.

rows.label: If rated F or G
rows.value: Mandatory decline unless evidenced improvement plan to reach E prior to letting (L)

rows.label: EPC validity period
rows.value: 10 years from date of issue — expiry date must be confirmed

rows.label: Source to obtain
rows.value: Estate agent particulars; MHCLG Energy Performance of Buildings Register

title: Rent comparables materially below stated figure — ICR fails at comp rent
description: The applicant has stated a rental income of £1,200/month. Local market comparables for the area range from £950–£1,050/month — approximately 14–21% below the stated figure. The valuer's market rent assessment (not yet received) will be the authoritative figure under §24.4, and ICR at Offer must use the lower of stated rent and valuer's assessment. The financial impact is significant. At the correct 5.50% stress rate (see sug_003), even the stated rent produces an ICR of 122.2% which already fails the 125% minimum for a Limited Company borrower. If the valuer assesses market rent at the midpoint of comparables (£1,000/month), the ICR falls to (£1,000 × 12) / (£214,200 × 5.50%) = £12,000 / £11,781 = 101.9% — a serious fail, approximately 23 percentage points below the minimum threshold. The case cannot proceed to Offer on current figures. The rental income position must be resolved by the valuer's assessment before any further progression is warranted.

rows.label: Policy — authoritative rent figure
rows.value: §24.4 — lower of stated rent or valuer's market rent assessment used at Offer

actions: Instruct valuation with market rent assessment
Do not proceed to Offer before valuer rent confirmed
Recalculate ICR when valuer report received
